known_bugs: Billing Core phase_010/run_attempt_014 is stale evidence rather than a live run: worker lifecycle shows T005-DEBUG-1 completed at 2026-06-26 18:15:10 +08:00, but state.json remained stamped at 2026-06-26 17:56:30 +08:00 with active_tasks still containing T005-DEBUG-1.
The old run_attempt_014 parent process used pre-V2.77/V2.78 controller logic, so its task-dispatch behavior should not be used to judge the current checkout.
T004 still needs a fresh Billing Core resume boundary under the current checkout to determine whether any remaining failure is unfinished repository work or another controller/runtime defect.
Historical upstream instability existed on 2026-06-25, but the latest 2026-06-26 network smoke passed; there is no current evidence that network availability is the blocking factor.

Billing Core real-worker shells still do not inherit a PATH entry for Go by default even though go.exe is installed at C:\Users\T14S\tools\go-1.26.3\go\bin\go.exe; recovery relaunches must inject that path or equivalent tool discovery until the environment is normalized.

Billing Core T004 remains the real current project blocker: run_attempt_015 stopped on non-partial blockers B-T004-2 and B-T004-3, with T005 and T005-DEBUG-1 still pending and intentionally undispatched.

Before V2.81, the full-roadmap executor treated all phase blockers as non-repairable, which prevented Alchemy from opening a new autonomous repair attempt after T004 technical blockers. V2.81 addresses this for implementation/test blockers while preserving stops for preflight, credentials, recovery, operator, and live-worker blockers.
Before V2.82, the full-roadmap resume scanner could fall back from a newer terminal run_attempt_015 to older stale run_attempt_014 active-task evidence. V2.82 prevents that fallback.

Before V2.83, Windows real Codex workers still treated workspace-write sessions as read-only and rejected patch/verification commands by policy; V2.83 uses the official Codex bypass flag for Windows workspace-write workers, disables plugin sync noise, and relies on Alchemy's isolated worktree plus file-boundary audit controls.

Before V2.84, a real worker timeout created a same-scope debug task, and a debug timeout reset the parent task for replay. Billing Core run_attempt_019 reproduced this as T002 timeout, T002-DEBUG-1 timeout, then T002 replay; V2.84 records a non-partial technical blocker instead.

Before V2.85, full-roadmap resume selection treated active-task attempts with terminal timeout lifecycle evidence as resumable if no worker PID was alive. V2.85 skips those stale terminal-active attempts and starts a fresh attempt.

Before V2.86, manifest-scoped frontend tasks could fail Alchemy boundary audit when dependency commands touched same-directory lockfiles such as frontend/pnpm-lock.yaml. V2.86 expands allowed_files from package.json to common lockfile companions.

Before V2.87, a manually stopped debug worker could leave active_tasks pointing at T002-DEBUG-1 with a dead running lifecycle record, causing the next Billing Core resume to replay stale debug work. V2.87 skips dead active debug attempts while preserving ordinary interrupted task resume.

As of 2026-06-27 02:31 +08:00, local Codex CLI real-model access is temporarily blocked by account usage limits. Real Alchemy workers use that Codex CLI login path, so launching Billing Core before the reset would create a false run failure.

Before V2.88, resuming a previously blocked full-roadmap phase could launch with only the original broad phase requirements instead of a repair brief seeded from the previous blocker evidence, making phase_010 feel like it was starting over.

Before V2.88, bare api key/auth non-repairable marker matching could misclassify CRM API-key or identity product workflow blockers as credential blockers. V2.88 narrows those markers to credential-shaped phrases.

Billing Core phase_010/run_attempt_023 is now cleanly blocked at T006: T001-T005 are completed, targeted frontend checks passed, and remaining failures are out-of-scope full-suite/typecheck issues that need narrower Alchemy repair tasks with expanded allowed files.

Before V2.89, repair narrative such as 'in allowed scope' could accidentally seed graph-wide allowed_prefixes, causing later 'previous relevant files' evidence to collapse a frontend closure phase back to old narrow files.

Before V2.89, frontend large_refactor planning was short-circuited by scoped file evidence before the frontend decomposition path, producing a backend strict T002 task for Billing Core phase_010 instead of focused frontend repair tasks.
Before V2.89, .vue file paths in repair evidence were not extracted, weakening handoff for Vue frontend failures such as AppSidebar, EmailVerifyView, UsageTable, and DashboardView.

Before V2.89, a supervisor-stopped bad attempt could still be considered by resume selection unless a newer terminal state superseded it; supervisor_stop.json/operator_stop.json are now explicit terminal attempt markers.

Timeout handling is safer after V2.84/V2.88, but still not progress-aware. Later optimization should add worker heartbeats, checkpoints, and bounded grace rather than simply raising max_worker_seconds.
Before V2.90, local Codex CLI usage-limit JSONL could be summarized as unparseable worker output, causing unnecessary debug/retry work and a false product repair phase.
As of 2026-06-27 17:02 +08:00, local Codex CLI real-model access was temporarily blocked by account usage limits until about 2026-06-27 17:39 +08:00; the 17:39 post-reset smoke passed.
Before V2.91, V2.90's usage-limit detector scanned the whole successful Codex JSONL stream and could misclassify historical quota text in repair evidence as a live Codex usage-limit blocker.

Before V2.92, a focused repair document could identify T003 retired API caller files in components, composables, and constants, but the rebuilt graph kept T003 on its old API-only scope and risked replaying the same non-partial blocker before later tasks could run.
Before V2.93, a T007 timeout repair document could advise splitting/checkpointing, but the rebuilt graph still contained the same broad copy/i18n worker scope and could replay the 900 second timeout.
